interview_name: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman

main_summary.summary: Joint oral history with David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman, classmates at Chicago Theological Seminary (CTS) who traveled to Selma, Alabama in March 1965 following Jesse Jackson's convocation appeal at the seminary in the days after Bloody Sunday. Satoko Ito Ackerman recounts her path from Kobe College in Nishinomiya, Japan to Yankton College in South Dakota (1959) through a Congregational Church scholarship, then to CTS for graduate work. David Ackerman recounts growing up in Mt. Carroll, Illinois, attending Knox College in Galesburg, and entering CTS after his brother's suicide during Ranger training at Fort Benning following the military's discovery that he was gay. Both describe Jesse Jackson as a CTS classmate, the convocation at which he commandeered the meeting to issue Dr. King's call for ministers, the twelve-person two-van caravan to Selma, sheltering with Black families in Selma, Dr. King's rally at Brown Chapel A.M.E. Church, the news of Jim Reeb's beating, the Thursday line-of-march arranged with Black women at the center, the all-night vigil after Jim Clark blocked the march at the Edmund Pettus Bridge approach, and the sunrise moment when troopers removed their helmets during the national anthem. The interview concludes with the Ackermans' move to Silver Spring, Maryland and a 1970s incident in which a white neighbor forbade his daughters from playing with a Black neighbor child.
key_themes: Chicago Theological Seminary and Jesse Jackson's convocation call
Selma to Montgomery voting rights campaign (March 1965)
Interfaith and interracial seminary response to Dr. King's appeal
Black community hospitality and protective vigil for visiting marchers
Continuing racial discrimination in Northern suburbs (Silver Spring, Maryland)
main_summary.historical_significance: The Ackermans' account documents the white seminary student response to Dr. King's 'all people of good will' call following Bloody Sunday, with first-person detail on how Jesse Jackson personally mobilized Chicago Theological Seminary students, the moral wrestling of fellow students (including a Texas classmate disowned by his family for considering the trip), and the racial geography of Selma observed by first-time visitors. Satoko Ito Ackerman's perspective as a Japanese-born student adds a non-Black, non-white view of the line-of-march arrangement (Black women centered, then Black men, then white women, then white men on the outside) and the protective shelter provided by Black host families who maintained overnight guard outside their homes. The interview preserves a specific recollection of Reverend Ralph Abernathy leading the all-night vigil at the bridge approach in Dr. King's absence, of Sheriff Jim Clark, and of the morning sunrise moment when Alabama troopers removed their helmets during the singing of the national anthem. David Ackerman's path from seminary to law school and the National Council of Churches' Washington office anti-poverty advocacy work, alongside the 1970s Silver Spring incident, situates Selma within a continuing civil rights trajectory rather than as a discrete event.

chapters.title: Satoko Ito's Path from Kobe to Yankton
chapters.summary: Satoko Ito Ackerman recounts her upbringing in Osaka and education at Kobe College in Nishinomiya, a women's mission school where she was introduced to English and Christianity. Through Angie Crew, a missionary, she received a scholarship from the South Dakota Conference of Congregational Churches to attend Yankton College, arriving in August 1959. After Yankton, she returned to Japan briefly before coming back through the Toko Fund for seminary graduate work.

chapters.title: David Ackerman's Mt. Carroll Childhood and Brother's Death
chapters.summary: David Ackerman describes growing up in Mt. Carroll, Illinois, a small farming town of two thousand people, and majoring in history and political science at Knox College in Galesburg. His senior year was marked by the suicide of his brother, who was in Ranger training at Fort Benning, Georgia when the military discovered he was gay and threatened him with dishonorable discharge. A friend's suggestion led David to Chicago Theological Seminary.

chapters.title: Awareness of Race Before Selma
chapters.summary: Both Ackermans describe how race entered their consciousness. Satoko recalls African students at Yankton College being refused haircuts in town but otherwise had no personal experience of discrimination. David recalls Mt. Carroll as a 'lily-white' community where one Black family was 'graciously received' but the issue rarely surfaced. At Chicago Theological Seminary, clinical activities took David into Woodlawn, the predominantly Black community across the Midway from the University of Chicago.
related_events: Seminary clinical work in Black Chicago

chapters.title: Jesse Jackson's Convocation Call
chapters.summary: David describes the weekly seminary convocation at which Jesse Jackson, then a CTS classmate already deeply involved in the Civil Rights Movement, commandeered the meeting to make Selma a personal moral issue. Dr. King had issued the 'all people of good will' call to ministers, and Jackson posed the question directly: he was going, who would join him. Both Ackermans recall initially perceiving Jackson as vain or self-important but seeing him become a leader that night with passion and integrity.

chapters.title: Deciding to Go and the Texas Classmate
chapters.summary: Satoko describes feeling personally called to go despite being a foreign student and a woman, and David's initial concern for her safety because of the prospect of violence. The chapter records the moral wrestling of the seminary community, including a friend from Amarillo, Texas who agonized over the decision and ultimately stayed behind because his parents would disown him and terminate his seminary funding. Seminary president Dr. Howard Schomer advised students not to go, though he was going himself.
related_events: Twelve seminarians decide to go to Selma

chapters.title: The Trip Down to Selma
chapters.summary: Twelve CTS students traveled in two vans on Tuesday afternoon, taking roughly 650 miles of two-lane road with no expressways to Selma. The most memorable incident was a gas station stop in Alabama where white men loitering at the station went to a payphone; Jesse Jackson recognized the threat, told the group to get in the cars, and they fled with a car following them for some distance. The group put on ministerial collars at the next stop for whatever protection they might provide.

chapters.title: Arrival in Selma and Brown Chapel Rally
chapters.summary: The Ackermans describe the stark racial geography of Selma — paved streets, sidewalks, and streetlights on the white side; none on the Black side. The group arrived at Brown Chapel A.M.E. Church, where a rally was building toward Dr. King's address. Brown Chapel was filled to the rafters with civil rights singing. News reached the gathering that Jim Reeb, a Unitarian minister who had answered Dr. King's call, had been brutally beaten on Tuesday night and was in critical condition.

chapters.title: Black Community Hospitality
chapters.summary: The chapter records the welcome from Brown Chapel community members, who fed and thanked the visiting marchers profusely. Satoko and her friend Ginny were hosted by two schoolteachers in a newly built home on the outskirts of Selma; the host family stayed guard outside the house through the night without telling the guests. David stayed in what seemed like public housing in the Black community and heard accounts of rapes and lynchings, recognizing that no functioning justice system protected Selma's Black residents.

chapters.title: Dr. King's Address and the Thursday March Formation
chapters.summary: Dr. King addressed the gathering at Brown Chapel, thanking those whose conscience had brought them to Selma and announcing that a court appearance would prevent him from leading the Thursday march in person; Reverend Ralph Abernathy would lead in his place. The marchers formed up on a dirt road outside the church in deliberate concentric formation: Black women at the center, Black men flanking them, then white women, then white men on the outside. Satoko describes the electricity of locked arms; David describes the fear of being assigned the 'place of honor' on the outside of the line.
related_events: Thursday march toward Edmund Pettus Bridge

chapters.title: Jim Clark, the All-Night Vigil, and the Helmets-Off Moment
chapters.summary: After three or four steps the marchers were halted by Sheriff Jim Clark of Dallas County. Reverend Abernathy led the group to its knees in prayer, and an all-night vigil followed, with sermons, songs, and first-person testimony from Selma's Black residents about voter-registration obstruction, sexual violence, and lynching. The Ackermans credit the television lights of the journalists as their 'guardian angel.' At sunrise, Abernathy led the singing of the national anthem; the troopers visibly hesitated before Jim Clark removed his helmet, and the others followed.

chapters.title: Community of Atonement and Long-View Reflection
chapters.summary: David characterizes the all-night vigil as an uplifting spiritual experience without emotional roller coaster; Satoko frames the experience as a 'community of atonement' (pronounced 'at-one-ment'), the theme of her seminary thesis on atoning community in nursery school children. The chapter records the breakfast with Jim Farmer of CORE that Jesse Jackson arranged on the Friday morning before the group's return to Chicago, and David's eventual path from seminary to law school, the National Council of Churches' Washington office, and congressional research service.
keywords: National Council of Churches Washington office
related_events: David Ackerman's transition to law and public service

chapters.title: Silver Spring and Continuing Struggle
chapters.summary: Satoko recounts a 1970s incident on Bradford Road in Silver Spring, Maryland, where a Black family with two children moved into their all-white neighborhood. After playing with the Ackermans' neighbors' daughters one day, the Black child Vera was told by the girls that their father had forbidden further play. Satoko confronted the girls and required that they play with everyone present when on the Ackerman yard. David closes with reflection on drug laws used against the Black community and new attempts to limit voting rights — 'somehow that boulder keeps having to be pushed up the mountain.'
related_events: Repeal of Don't Ask Don't Tell (interview-day context)

loc_video.caption: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman oral history interview conducted by Joseph Mosnier in Washington, D.C., 2011 September 20
